concepts.name: Value Network
concepts.definition: The context within which a firm identifies customers, solves problems, procures inputs, responds to competitors, and seeks profit. It defines which performance attributes matter, which cost structures are acceptable, and which opportunities look valuable.

concepts.name: Impetus to Innovate
concepts.definition: The pressure to invest in innovations that improve performance for the firm's current customers and preserve margins inside its existing value network.

concepts.name: Sustaining vs. Disruptive Innovation
concepts.definition: Sustaining innovations improve products along dimensions current customers value; disruptive innovations initially underperform on those dimensions but offer a different value package attractive to a different or lower-end network.

arguments.claim: Incumbents prioritize sustaining innovations for structural reasons, not because managers are irrational.
arguments.evidence: Customers in the value network ask for better performance, margins are higher in established tiers, and resource allocation naturally favors projects that satisfy powerful customers.

A small drive, hydraulic excavator, or mini-mill output can look unattractive in one network and valuable in another; the same technical artifact is interpreted differently by different customers and cost structures.

cases.name: Disk-drive form factors
cases.context: Smaller drives initially had lower capacity than mainstream customers needed.
cases.illustrates: Why incumbents rationally ignored them in their existing network while entrants found new networks that valued size and power advantages.
cases.lesson: Ask which network values the new attribute, not whether today's best customers want it.

logicChains.description: Current customers define valued performance → resource allocation favors sustaining projects → disruptive projects look low-margin or irrelevant → entrants serve another network → performance improves → entrant invades the mainstream.

terminology.definition: A firm's embedded market, cost, and customer context.
Initially inferior mainstream performance with a different value proposition in a foothold network.

nuance: Listening to customers is good management in sustaining competition and a trap in disruption.
The theory explains rational failure; it does not blame managers for being foolish.
